title: Unprivileged groups are less served by green cooling services in major European urban areas
methods: Pearson correlation of cooling vs socioeconomic indicators

data_scope.spatial_coverage: 14 major European urban areas (functional urban areas)
data_scope.socioeconomic_indicators: income, tenure/homeownership, immigration status, unemployment (Esri Living Atlas / national census)
data_scope.note: exact per-city population samples and EC-tower counts are in Extended Data tables, not in public metadata

core_conclusion: Across 14 major European urban areas, the cooling delivered by urban green space is distributed unjustly: vulnerable residents cluster in run-down central districts with the lowest cooling, not the suburbs. Lower-income residents, tenants, immigrants and the unemployed receive below-average green cooling while upper-income residents, nationals and homeowners receive above-average cooling, amplifying heat-related fatality risk for those least able to afford other cooling.
why_it_matters: Heat is Europe's deadliest climate hazard; the paper recasts green cooling as a life-or-death public-health service that is systematically maldistributed, overturns the 'vulnerable suburbs' intuition for Europe, moves from greenness counts to a physically modelled 10-m cooling service, and shows the injustice is consistent across all 14 cities.
why_nature: Couples physical climate modelling with fine-grained socioeconomic equity data; transferable pattern across a continent rather than a single case; directly policy-actionable for greening and heat adaptation; releases open, reproducible 10-m GCoS maps and the SCOPE/rSCOPE code.
added_value_to_my_research: Recast green cooling as accessible supply via 2SFCA/R2SFCA over the pedestrian network
Explainable ML/SHAP and MGWR to disentangle socioeconomic vs morphological drivers of cooling
Efficiency-vs-equity facility-location to prioritize greening for below-average groups
Distill SCOPE into a fine-tuned geospatial foundation model for global GCoS inference
Fuse cooling deficit with healthcare/telehealth access into a compound heat-health vulnerability index
